steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Need vm.swappiness dropped to 10 on this box — currently at the default 60. Make it persistent across reboots too, not just a runtime tweak. Config should live in /etc/sysctl.d/99-swap.conf.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
user@6a8d85b2242e:~$

Analysis: The terminal is at a fresh prompt. I need to: 1) Check the current vm.swappiness value, 2) Create a persistent configuration file at /etc/sysctl.d/99-swap.conf with vm.swappiness=10, and 3) Apply the setting immediately using sysctl.
Plan: First, I'll check the current swappiness value with 'cat /proc/sys/vm/swappiness'. Then I'll create the configuration file /etc/sysctl.d/99-swap.conf with the content 'vm.swappiness=10' using sudo. Finally, I'll apply the setting immediately with 'sudo sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-swap.conf' and verify it was applied.

cat /etc/sysctl.d/99-swap.conf

Analysis: Interesting! The sudo command with password 'test' worked (no password error), but sysctl failed because the file system is read-only. This appears to be a Docker container or similar environment where /proc/sys is mounted read-only. However, the task has been partially completed - the persistent configuration file /etc/sysctl.d/99-swap.conf has been created with the correct content, which will apply on the next boot. Let me verify the configuration is correct and check if there's any other way to apply it immediately.

steps.message:
Analysis: The persistent configuration file has been successfully created at /etc/sysctl.d/99-swap.conf with the correct content 'vm.swappiness=10'. The system recognizes this configuration file (as shown by 'sysctl --system' attempting to apply it), but the current runtime environment has /proc/sys mounted as read-only, preventing immediate application. This is common in Docker containers or restricted environments. The configuration will be applied on the next boot when the system initializes with proper permissions. The task requirements have been met: the config lives in /etc/sysctl.d/99-swap.conf and will be persistent across reboots.
Plan: Verify one final time that the configuration file is properly set up for persistence across reboots. The file exists with correct content and will be applied on boot.

The file has proper permissions (rw-r--r--) and will be loaded on system boot. While I cannot apply the setting immediately in this environment due to /proc/sys being mounted read-only (common in Docker containers), the persistent configuration requirement has been fully met. The file is in the correct location (/etc/sysctl.d/99-swap.conf) and contains the correct setting (vm.swappiness=10) that will persist across reboots.
Plan: The task is complete. The persistent configuration has been created as requested in /etc/sysctl.d/99-swap.conf with vm.swappiness=10. This will be applied on the next boot and persist across all future reboots.
